levels.body: These are two kinds of precious stones. Whereas “amethyst” is a “[q]uartz of a violet or purple variety” (“amethyst, *n*. and *adj*.” A.1.a.), “beryl” is described as having “a pale-green colour passing into light-blue, yellow, and white; distinguished only by colour from the more precious emerald” (“beryl, *n*. and *adj*.” A.I.1.a.).
worksCited: “amethyst, *n*. and *adj*.” *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, December 2021, www.oed.com/view/Entry/6366. Accessed 2 December 2021.
“beryl, *n*. and *adj*.” *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, December 2021, www.oed.com/view/Entry/18007. Accessed 2 December 2021.

levels.body: The stones are part of a simile. They are compared to the grapes of the previous line whose destruction the speaker would accept as a consequence of change (cf. [[storm]]).

The simile highlights the colour and, potentially, ripeness, of the grapes, creating a mental image of shiny purple and green grapes mixed together. The comparison intimates the island’s lushness and fertility.
